You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.
- Do not include `references[]` — that gets added by a later step
  pointing back at chapter files.

# Book-level distillation — method-vol-1-chord-construction

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc

The George Benson Method, Vol. 1 is structured as a strict bottom-up curriculum that moves from notation literacy to a working theory of diatonic harmony on the guitar. Chapter 1 establishes the notation vocabulary: the staff, the treble/bass/C clef family (with the treble G-clef privileged for guitar), and the grand staff. Chapter 2 builds the pitch and interval system on top of that notation - the diatonic and chromatic scales, the W-W-H-W-W-W-H major formula, the relative minor as the 6th-degree rotation, and a full interval taxonomy (melodic/harmonic, simple/compound, diatonic/chromatic, enharmonic). Chapter 3 turns those scales into a system of key relationships via the Circle of Fourths and Fifths, with explicit memorization anchors (orders of sharps and flats) and mirror-image identification rules. Chapter 4 - the densest chapter - constructs the chord universe by tertian stacking. Chapter 5 organizes those chords into harmonic fields and the seven modes, Chapter 6 prescribes how to practice those harmonic fields, and Chapter 7 extends the field idea to Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major as color sources rather than progression engines. The pedagogical sequence is itself a thesis: notation, then scales, then key relationships, then chords, then fields, then practice method, then chromatic color.

## The Fretboard-as-Matrix System

The book's load-bearing system is a chord-first visualization of the fretboard. Chapter 4 declares the fretboard a matrix to be navigated chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases derived from chord knowledge rather than the reverse. Chord shapes are organized into four root-string zones - 6th, 5th, 4th, and (for triads only) 3rd string - and Chapter 2 supplies the spatial scaffolding for this: the Root splits the fretboard into left and right sides, the fretboard repeats at the 12th fret, and two Guide Notes on the 5th and 6th strings serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape. Chapter 2 also issues the methodological rule that shapes and intervals must be learned together, never separately. Chapters 6 and 7 then restate a universal practice rule across every harmonic field and every derived family: locate the chord's bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings first, and defer 4th-string voicings until those are mastered.

## The Chord-Family System (Chapter 4)

Chapter 4 partitions the chord universe into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). The signature device is the Chord Skeleton: pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants. Triads have four qualities and three inversions (root, first, second), with the rule that diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric. Tetrads expand to Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework; an unmarked 7 is minor by convention (major 7 must be explicitly indicated); Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations are restricted to tetrads; and enharmonic identities are formalized (C6 = Am7 first inversion; Cm(b6) = Abmaj7 first inversion). Extended Chords (9, 11, 13) codify the #11-versus-natural-11 rule (use #11 on major and dominant chords to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely), define altered chords as dominants with more than one altered extension, and note that the 5th is almost always omitted in 13ths. Voice leading is governed by a smallest-movement rule, and a specific voicing prohibition forbids a flat-9 between bass and top note on major or minor chords. Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2, sus4) and are taught by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes. A load-bearing instrument constraint runs throughout: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes, and this drives nearly every voicing choice.

## The Harmonic Field System (Chapters 5-6)

The harmonic field is the book's organizing frame for harmony in practice. Chapter 5 builds it by stacking thirds on each degree of the natural major scale, yielding seven diatonic chords in Roman numerals (I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm), and pairs each degree with one of the seven modes (Ionian through Locrian) as rotations of the parent scale. The book's central rule here is that modes drive both harmony and melody by revealing each chord's tensions and the correct scale choice. Chapter 5 then introduces a four-category taxonomy of scale notes that becomes load-bearing for the rest of the book: Chord Notes (1-3-5(-7)), Extended Notes (9, 11, 13 tensions), Avoid Notes (usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones), and Characteristic Notes (those that give the mode its identity). It distinguishes the Natural Major and Natural Minor Harmonic Fields as the 'happy' and 'sad' frames sharing chords but differing in analytic vantage, and clarifies that listed extensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components. Chapter 6 then prescribes how to study these fields: a four-rule Study Devices protocol (one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before key changes, 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string), the Visualization via bass note principle, and a degree-by-degree substitution catalog (e.g., the V dominant-to-sus substitution G7 -> Gsus9/Gsus13, the IIIm phrygian Em7(b6) and slash voicings, the VIIm locrian recast of Bo as G7(9)/B with the 9th forbidden because it produces a b9 avoid note).

## Color Scales and the Leading-Tone Argument (Chapter 7)

Chapter 7 grounds the rest of the book's chromatic vocabulary in a single functional definition: the leading tone is the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale, the half-step tension the Natural Minor lacks. This historical absence motivated raising the natural minor's VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, then the Melodic Minor Scale (treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form rather than the older ascending-only convention), and finally Rimsky-Korsakov's Harmonic Major Scale. Each parent scale generates seven scale modes - the book deliberately calls these scale modes rather than harmonic fields, because the rule is that their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices layered over existing chords. Signature derived modes are spelled out: from Melodic Minor, the VII Altered Scale, bIII Lydian Augmented, and IV Lydian Dominant; from Harmonic Major, bVI Lydian Augmented (#2/#5) and VIIm Locrian (bb7), the latter sourcing o7(b13) and (11) colors. A recurring VERY IMPORTANT rule restates that extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.

## The Method as a Unit

Taken together, the volume teaches an integrated method with four interlocking commitments. First, terminology is fixed and load-bearing: harmonic field, diatonic chords, Degrees, the seven mode names, the Chord/Extended/Avoid/Characteristic note categories, Chord Skeletons, Guide Notes, Drop 2/Drop 3, leading tone, scale modes, Bach Scale. Second, the fretboard is taught chord-first with bass-note visualization on the 5th and 6th strings, 4th-string voicings deferred, and shapes-with-intervals learned as a single unit. Third, practice is rule-governed: one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before modulation, and proximity-based chord selection. Fourth, the analytic frame separates structural diatonic harmony (the natural major and minor harmonic fields of Chapters 5-6) from chromatic color (the derived scale-mode families of Chapter 7), with explicit rules - the smallest-movement voice-leading rule, the #11-versus-11 rule, the no-flat-9-between-bass-and-top rule, the sus-as-3rd-replacement rule, the no-inversions-for-symmetric-chords rule, the VII-9 forbidden in Locrian, and the omit-the-5th-in-13ths convention - constraining every voicing choice. The instrument constraint that the guitar must edit and omit notes, stated in Chapter 4, sits behind the whole method and explains why visualization, families, and bass-note anchoring carry the pedagogical weight that piano-centric theory would assign to literal voice-leading.

# Chapter 2 — Musical Scales & Intervals

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 lays the theoretical and notational groundwork the rest of the book depends on, beginning with the diatonic scale built on the seven natural notes with half steps between B-C and E-F, the major-scale formula W-W-H-W-W-W-H, the natural (relative) minor taken from the 6th degree with its W-H-W-W-H-T-T pattern, and the 12-note chromatic universe. It then codifies the book's diagram conventions: left-hand finger numbering 1-4, the X (muted) and O (open) symbols, fret-number indicators tied to the nearest finger, arched-line barres with repeated-number notation for non-index barres, and the rule that scale diagrams show intervals over a chosen root rather than fingerings. A spatial framework is introduced in which the Root splits the fretboard into left and right sides, the fretboard repeats at the 12th fret, and notes are memorized in steps using open strings, naturals on the 6th and 5th strings, and octave shortcuts to the 4th, 3rd, and 2nd strings (with the B-string anomaly handled via a 4th-string octave). A core methodological rule is stated explicitly under the Shapes vs intervals warning - shapes and intervals must be learned together, anchored by two Guide Notes on the 5th and 6th strings that serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape. The chapter then defines the load-bearing interval vocabulary: melodic vs harmonic, simple vs compound, diatonic vs chromatic, and enharmonic intervals, preparing the reader for later chord-spelling and alteration work. Ear training is prescribed via the song-reference method, with complete ascending and descending interval tables (24 songs total, from Isn't She Lovely through Willow Weep for Me) and a full inversion table mapping each ascending interval to its descending complement, foundational for chord inversions and voice leading.

# Chapter 3 — Circle of Fourth / Circle of Fifth

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the Circle of Fourths and Circle of Fifths as the system of relationships among major and minor keys on the Western chromatic scale, anchored in the C Natural Major Scale and its W-W-H-W-W-W-H interval pattern. Under "Preserving major-scale sound," it frames sharps and flats not as arbitrary accidentals but as modifications required to preserve that pattern in other keys, and then enumerates the canonical sequences: C, G, D, A, E, B, F#, C# for the sharp (fifth) circle and C, F, Bb, Eb, Ab, Db, Gb, Cb for the flat (fourth) circle. The chapter drills the order of sharps (F, C, G, D, A, E, B) and flats (B, E, A, D, G, C, F) as a memorization anchor, then gives two mirror-image identification rules with worked examples: the last sharp is a half step below the major key (three sharps ending on G# yields A major), while for flats the one before the last is the key (three flats ending on Ab gives Eb major). It also covers notational mechanics under "Key signature placement" and "Canceling a key change" — the signature sits between clef and time signature, and modulations are written by naturalizing the old accidentals before introducing the new signature. Finally, under "Major vs minor and relative key," it introduces the load-bearing term relative key, defined as the VI degree of the major and characterized as the "sad" minor counterpart to each major signature.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 builds the book's central thesis that the fretboard is a matrix to be visualized chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases flowing out of chord knowledge organized into four root-string zones (6th, 5th, 4th, and 3rd strings, the last only for triads). Under Triads, the chapter gives interval formulas for the four qualities, names the three inversion positions (root, first, second), introduces slash-chord notation (e.g., C/E), and presents the book's signature 'Chord Skeletons' - pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants, plus playability caveats (e.g., avoid the 6th-string left-side minor shape) and the rule that diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric. Tetrad Chords expand to Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework, the convention that an unmarked 7 is minor (major 7 must be indicated), enharmonic equivalences (C6 = Am7/first-inversion; Cm(b6) = Abmaj7/first-inversion), and Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations restricted to tetrads. Voice leading is governed by the smallest-movement rule, and characteristic voicing prohibitions appear - notably avoiding a flat-9 between bass and top note on major/minor chords. Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2/sus4), and the chapter recommends visualizing them by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes, decoding jazz symbols like Gsus9 and Gsus13 into precise tone content. Extended Chords cover 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths as tertian-with-exceptions, codifying the #11-vs-natural-11 rule (use #11 on major and dominant 7 to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely), defining altered chords as dominants with more than one altered extension, and noting that the 5th is almost always omitted in 13th chords. The chapter also maps 4th-string extended voicings as derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes (typically starting from the 7th or 6th of the chord) and surfaces the dominant-7 web of enharmonic relationships with diminished, half-diminished, and maj7(#4) chords. A load-bearing instrument constraint runs throughout: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes, which drives nearly every voicing choice in the chapter.

# Chapter 6 — How to Study the Harmonic Fields

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 lays out a practice methodology and substitution catalog for studying the diatonic harmonic fields on guitar, opening with a four-rule Study Devices protocol (one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before key changes, and 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string ones) and a Visualization via bass note principle that anchors chord study to roots on the 5th and 6th strings rather than to scale shapes. The chapter closes on the VIIm locrian avoid-note rule, recasting Bo as G7(9)/B and forbidding the 9th on the VII chord because it produces a b9 avoid note. # Chapter 7 — Leading Tone & How Other Scales Appeared

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 builds its entire framework on the functional definition of the leading tone as the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale, the half-step tension that the Natural Minor lacked and that historically motivated raising its VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, followed by the Melodic Minor Scale (originally ascending-only, now treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form) and Rimsky-Korsakov's mid-twentieth-century Harmonic Major Scale. These three new families each generate seven scale modes (deliberately called scale modes rather than harmonic fields), and the chapter is emphatic that their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices that add tension on top of existing chords. Load-bearing interval spellings are given for each parent mode (Im Harmonic Minor with min 3rd/min 6th/maj 7th, Im Melodic Minor with min 3rd/maj 6th/maj 7th, I Harmonic Major as major scale with b6) and for the signature derived modes the families produce: the VII Altered Scale, bIII Lydian Augmented, and IV Lydian Dominant from Melodic Minor, plus Harmonic Major's bVI Lydian Augmented (#2/#5) and VIIm Locrian (bb7), the latter sourcing o7(b13) and (11) colors. Each mode is specified by avoid notes and characteristic notes (e.g., Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th), and a recurring VERY IMPORTANT rule clarifies that extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord. The master's characteristic moves are pedagogical: a universal fretboard-visualization rule restated for each family (locate the chord's bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings only, with 4th-string chords deferred until comfort), plus the study devices of practicing harmonic fields in the same key, always with a rhythmic feel (funk, swing), and choosing chords close to one another for proximity. The terminology this chapter makes load-bearing is leading tone, scale modes, derived chord families as color, avoid/characteristic notes, Bach Scale, Altered, Lydian Augmented, Lydian Dominant, and Locrian bb7.

# Chapter 3 — Circle of Fourth / Circle of Fifth

_Pages 47-51._

## Circle of 4th / 5th definition

> The circle of fourth (or circle of fifth) is the relationship among the major and the minor keys
> existing on the Western chromatic scale. It is important to understand that everything starts with
> the “C” Natural Major Scale: C, D, E, F, G, A, B, C which has the pattern W-W-H-W-W-W-H
> (W = whole tone / H = half tone ).

**Significance (p. 28).** Establishes the foundational major scale interval pattern that every other key derivation in the chapter depends on.

## Preserving major-scale sound

> To have the same sound of “C” major in other scales, we have to modify some notes to keep the
> melodic pattern W-W-H-W-W-W-H.

**Significance (p. 28).** Explains why sharps and flats exist in other keys, framing accidentals as preservation of an interval pattern rather than arbitrary marks.

## Sharp and flat circles enumerated

> The Circles of 4th and 5th are defined according to the order that Sharps and Flats appear on
> the scales. Below is the list of all major scales: C, G, D, A, E, B, F#, C# (circle of fifth or sharp
> circle) and C, F, Bb, Eb, Ab, Db, Gb, Cb (circle of fourth or flat circle).

**Significance (p. 28).** Lists the canonical sequences of keys for both circles, the load-bearing reference data for the chapter.

## Order of sharps and flats

> SHARP ORDER: F, C , G , D , A , E , B                      FLAT ORDER: B , E , A , D , G , C , F

**Significance (p. 31).** Memorization anchor; the two ordered sequences underlie every key-signature identification rule taught next.

## Identifying key from sharps

> Memorizing the order of sharps and flats helps us realize quickly how many of them exist in
> every key. The secret of sharps is that the last one is a half step below from the major key.

**Significance (p. 31).** Gives the practical rule for naming a sharp key from its signature, a core sight-reading skill.

## Sharp-key identification example

> • What is the key that has three sharps? If we know their order, everything is easy - F#, C#, G#.
> The last sharp is " G# ", which is a half step below from A. Therefore, the key will be A major.

**Significance (p. 31).** Worked example showing how the half-step-below rule is applied to identify a sharp key.

## Identifying key from flats

> But the secret of flats is that the one before the last will be the key. Example:
>
> • What is the key that has 3 flats? The order is Bb, Eb, Ab. "Eb" is the one before the last flat,
> therefore it is the key.

**Significance (p. 31).** The mirror rule for flat keys, paired with a worked example; both rules together cover all key-signature reading.

## Key signature placement

> The key signature is placed on the staff after the clef and before the time signature. It
> determines the key of the song.

**Significance (p. 31).** Defines where key signatures live on the staff, a notational rule the reader needs before interpreting any score.

## Canceling a key change

> However, if there is a change of key in a song, we should cancel the sharps or flats on the
> previous key using the natural sign, and soon after, put the new key signature.

**Significance (p. 31).** Specifies the notational procedure for mid-piece key changes, a concrete rule for writing or reading modulations.

## Major vs minor and relative key

> A song can be in a major key (happy sound) or a minor key (sad sound) with some exceptions
> that we will learn later. We call the relative key those that sound "sad", and it is the VI degree
> in relation to the major key.

**Significance (p. 32).** Defines relative minor as the VI degree of the major, the key concept linking each major signature to its minor counterpart.

# Chapter 4 — Chord Structures

_Pages 52-195._

## Fretboard as matrix; chord-first method

> The guitar fretboard is a kind of matrix that we must visualize very well. In order to master
>  it, the <irst step is to learn as much as we can about many different chords in different zones.
>  These chords will guide us to learn everything we need to know, including arpeggios, scales
>  and phrases.

**Significance (p. 52).** States the book's organizing thesis: chord visualization across the fretboard is the gateway to scales, arpeggios, and phrases.

>  c) Chords starting from the 4th string
>  d) Chords starting from the 3rd string (works only with triads)

**Significance (p. 54).** Defines the theoretical scaffolding (stacked thirds) that justifies the chord-construction logic.

>                                 46
>      4) augmented triad = root, major 3rd, augmented 5th.

> Every dominant chord with more than one altered extended note is called an altered chord.

**Significance (p. 126).** Concise definition of the altered chord category.

## 4th-string chords derived from 5th/6th

> The extended chords will be learned by rooting them on the 5th and 6th strings. The 4th
> string will be used to play the 6th and 5th string chords without the bass note. Usually, a
> 4th string chord is derived from a 6th string chord. It will start from the 7th or 6th of the
> chord.

**Significance (p. 127).** Explains the derivation method that lets the player generate 4th-string extended voicings from known root-string shapes.

## Dominant 7 to diminished/half-dim/maj7#4 web

> Almost every 4th string chord can be used and played on the 5th string. By doing this we will
>   see some relationships between many different types of chords. As we have already learned the
>   connection between major and minor chords, it is time to concentrate on dominant 7th chords,
>   which have a strong relationship with diminished, half diminished and major 7th (#4) chords.

**Significance (p. 128).** Names the family of enharmonic substitutions that drive the chapter's altered-dominant material.

## Guitar must omit notes; not piano

> It is important to remember that many of the chords on the guitar have to be edited. This
>      is totally different from the piano which is a complete harmonic instrument, where we can have
>      many chord voicing possibilities without having to omit any note.

**Significance (p. 112).** States the load-bearing instrument-specific constraint that drives voicing choices throughout the chapter.

## 13th chords almost omit the 5th

> (Note: Notice that in
>
>                                     68

**Significance (p. 126).** A characteristic voicing rule for 13th chords.

# Chapter 5 — Harmonic Fields & Scale Modes

_Pages 196-205._

## Harmonic Field definition

> As we know, the majority of the chords are built from the superimposition of 3rd 's, and in this
>   chapter we will apply that concept over the Natural Major Scale. In other words, we will build
>   chords from each note from that scale forming what we call: diatonic chords. As we have 7
>   notes, we will have 7 chords as well.

**Significance (p. 177).** Defines how the harmonic field is constructed via tertian stacking on each scale degree, yielding seven diatonic chords.

## Roman numeral degrees

> Each chord, using the same order that they appear (triad or tetrad), is represented by a
>   roman numeral (upper-case numerals represent major chords, and lower-case numerals
>   represent minor chords): I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm. Now that we organized the chords
>   into roman numerals, we will call them “Degrees".

**Significance (p. 177).** Establishes the upper/lowercase Roman numeral convention used throughout the book for diatonic analysis.

## The 7 Modes

> If we start the natural major scale each time from different note, we will have 7 different scales.
> We call them “The 7 Modes”. Each mode will receive a specific name and has its own
> harmonic characteristics.

**Significance (p. 178).** Defines modes as rotations of the natural major scale, anchoring the book's modal framework.

## Mode names per degree

> I = Ionian: C, D, E, F, G, A, B
>
> II = Dorian : D, E, F, G, A, B, C
>
> III = Phrygian: E, F, G, A, B, C, D
>
> IV = Lydian: F, G, A, B, C, D, E
>
> V = Mixolydian: G, A, B, C, D, E, F
>
> VI = Aeolian: A, B, C, D, E, F, G
>
> VII = Locrian: B, C, D, E, F, G, A

**Significance (p. 178).** Load-bearing reference mapping each degree to its mode and exact note set in C.

## Modes drive harmony and melody

> It is important to understand that from now on we will use the modes for everything, including
> harmony and melody, because they can show us all the possible tensions for each chord and
> also which is the correct scale for each situation. So, you must memorize the names and the
> scales notes from each one of the modes.

**Significance (p. 178).** States the book's central rule that modes determine both chord tensions and scale choice.

## Types of Notes in the Modes

> a) Chord Notes:
>                        46
>    - Triads (1, 3, 5) or Tetrads (1, 3, 5, 7).
>
> b) Extended Notes:
>   - Tension notes or coloring notes (9, 11, 13).
>
> c) Avoid Notes:
>   - Scale notes that cause harmonic conflict with the chord notes. Melodically they can sound
>     interesting in the middle of a melody, but never as an ending note.
>
> d) Characteristic Notes:
>   - Scale notes that give the mode it's characteristics.

**Significance (p. 178).** Four-category taxonomy of scale notes used to analyze every mode in the book.

## Avoid notes rule

> Scale notes that cause harmonic conflict with the chord notes. Melodically they can sound
>     interesting in the middle of a melody, but never as an ending note.

**Significance (p. 178).** Practical rule for avoid notes: usable mid-phrase, never as a resting tone.

## Major vs Minor Harmonic Fields

> 1) Natural Major Harmonic Field (also can be called Major Harmonic Field)
>
>
>   2) Natural Minor Harmonic Field (also can be called Minor Harmonic Field)

**Significance (p. 179).** Names the two harmonic-field organizing systems analyzed in the chapter.

## Relative major/minor sharing chords

> Summarizing, we will use the Natural Major Harmonic Field to organize the
>   “happy sounding songs” and the Natural Minor Harmonic Field to organize the “sad sounding
>   songs”. In the following pages, we will better understand that both harmonic fields use the same
>   chords, the only difference is the way you analyze them.

**Significance (p. 179).** Key conceptual rule: major and minor fields share chords; only the analytic frame differs.

## Accidentals on degrees

> Note: When necessary we can use sharps (#) or flats (b) together with the degrees.
>   Example: bII, bIII, #IV.....

**Significance (p. 179).** Establishes notation convention for chromatic Roman numeral degrees used later.

## Ionian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, major 7th
>       Extended notes: major 9th, perfect 11th, major 13th
>       Chord: Cmaj 7 , C 69
>                    9
>       Avoid notes: perfect 4th /octave when the chord is Cmaj7
>       Characteristic notes: perfect 4th /major 7th

**Significance (p. 181).** Reference card for Ionian's chord tones, tensions, avoid notes, and characteristic notes.

## Lydian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, major 7th
>       Extended notes: major 9th, augmented 11th, major 13th
>       Chord: Fmaj7Ã   , Fmaj 79
>       Avoid notes: octave on Fmaj7 chords
>       Characteristic notes: augmented 4th /major 7th

**Significance (p. 181).** Defines Lydian's #11 tension and characteristic augmented 4th that distinguishes it from Ionian.

## Mixolydian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, minor 7th
> Extended notes: major 9th, perfect 11th, major 13th
> Chord: G 713 ( 9 ) ,   GW9
> Avoid notes: perfect 4th
> Characteristic notes: perfect 4th/minor 7th

**Significance (p. 182).** Defines dominant-seventh mode profile and its avoid-note constraint on the perfect 4th.

## Locrian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, minor 3rd, dim 5th, minor 7th
> Extended notes: minor 9th, perfect 11th, minor 13th
> Chord: Bª
> Avoid notes : minor 2nd
> Characteristic notes: minor 2nd /dim 5th /minor 7th

**Significance (p. 182).** Defines the half-diminished mode profile used for the VII chord and minor II analyses.

## Extended notes usage rule

> Not all extended notes of a chord must be in it.
>
> Many of them should only be played as the passing tones of a melody on the chord.

**Significance (p. 182).** Key rule clarifying that listed tensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components.

## Natural Minor Harmonic Field degrees

> DEGREE :          Im               IIm           bIII            IVm           Vm        bVI     bVII
>
>       TETRAD :          Ami7              Bª            Cmaj7          Dmi7          Emi7      Fmaj7    G7

**Significance (p. 183).** Authoritative degree-to-tetrad mapping for the natural minor harmonic field.

# Chapter 6 — How to Study the Harmonic Fields

_Pages 206-241._

## Study Devices for Harmonic Fields

> a) Play the harmonic /ields in the same key until you feel comfortable.
>   b) Always study with rhythm (funk, swing, etc). It helps you to memorize things faster.
>   c) Before you change or add a new key, make sure you know the key you practiced previously.
>   d) To make visualization easier, choose chords that are close to each other, using only the 6th
>   and 5th string chords. The 4th string chords should be studied only after you are comfortable
>   with the others.

**Significance (p. 187).** Lays out the four-rule practice protocol that governs how the entire harmonic field chapter must be drilled.

## Visualization via bass note

> The best way to not get lost on the guitar fretboard, is to 5irst visualize the bass note of the
>   chord. However, different from scales, we must visualize the root of the chord (bass note)
>   only using the 5th and 6th strings.

**Significance (p. 187).** Defines the chord-root visualization rule that distinguishes chord study from scale study on the fretboard.

## Ist degree: C6 vs Cmaj7

> We can replace C6 by Cmaj7 or vice-versa almost all the time. The only exception is when
> the melody is the octave. In this case we must use C6, in order to not create a con<lict
> between the melody and the chord (a b9 interval). An important thing to know is that we
> rarely use the perfect 4th over an Ionian chord. However we have some exceptions, where
> we can <ind chords with 4ths and 3rds or even omitted 3rd chords.

**Significance (p. 200).** States the substitution rule and the b9-conflict exception governing the Ionian (I) chord choices.

## IIm degree dorian replacements

> We can replace Dm7(9) dorian by Dm7(11) or even Dm7(13). In theory, we could also use
> Dm6, but minor 6th chords are more complex than they look, so we will come back to them
> later. For now, let’s consider that they can sometimes replace a minor 7th chord.

**Significance (p. 204).** Establishes which extended dorian voicings are interchangeable on the II degree and flags m6 as a deferred topic.

## IIIm degree phrygian options

> We can often add a +lat 6th in the IIIm7 degree. Example: Em7 can be replaced by Em7(b6),
>   Cmaj7/E or even C/E, which are the same chords. However, as a phrygian chord, Em7 can
>   also be replaced by Esus(b9) and sometimes Em7(b9), which was often used by composers
>   such as Antonio Carlos Jobim and also Heitor Villa Lobos.

**Significance (p. 207).** Names the b6 addition and slash-chord equivalences for the III degree and the phrygian sus(b9) substitution.

## IV degree lydian #4 rule

> We do not have to put the (#4) in the chord all the time, but in a few cases it might work. In
>  theory, G/F can be considered an F Lydian chord, including G7(13) or G7(9).

**Significance (p. 209).** Clarifies that the lydian #4 is optional, and identifies slash voicings that imply F lydian.

## V degree dominant sus substitution

> We can replace the V chord (dominant) with a sus chord. Example: G7 can be replaced by
>   Gsus9 or Gsus13. In theory, every Dm/G can be considered as a mixolydian chord even
>   without the note B, which is the 3rd of G.

**Significance (p. 213).** Defines the V-to-sus substitution and the Dm/G mixolydian reinterpretation, central to dominant function harmony.

## VIm degree aeolian options

> We can replace Am7(9) Aeolian with Am7(11) or even Am7(b6). But just like any degree,
>   the characteristic notes do not have to be in the chord all the time. In theory, every Dm7/A
>   or Fmaj7/A can be considered as an Aeolian chord.

**Significance (p. 217).** Lists the extended aeolian replacements and slash-chord aeolian voicings for the VI degree.

## VIIm locrian avoid-note rule

> Bø can be replaced with G7/B or even G/B. This happens frequently because we consider
>   the Bø as being G7(9)/B. Another important thing is that the VII degree cannot use the 9th
>   in the chord, because it would be ?lat 9th, which does not work properly for this type of
>   chord. Remember that the ?lat 9th is an avoid note on locrian chord.

**Significance (p. 221).** Captures the locrian substitution to G7/B and the b9 avoid-note rule that constrains VII-degree voicings.

# Chapter 7 — Leading Tone & How Other Scales Appeared

_Pages 242-311._

## Leading tone definition

> A leading tone
>   is what we call the note that resolves (or leads to) another tone a half step above or a half step
>   below it. However, functionally speaking it is the VII degree of the diatonic scale (Natural
>   Major Scale). Therefore, the note "B" is the leading tone of the "C" note which gives us the
>   feeling of tension and preparation for this tone.

**Significance (p. 223).** The whole chapter's framework rests on this functional definition of leading tone as the VII degree producing tension-and-resolution.

## Origin: Natural Minor had no leading tone

> One of the greatest musical problems for the church
>   choir at the time was to be able to sing the Natural Minor Scale in tune and this happened for
>   one reason: the Natural Minor Scale had no leading key.

**Significance (p. 223).** Historical premise that motivates the entire derivation of harmonic/melodic minor scales.

## Birth of Harmonic Minor Scale

> So, to end this problem, the VII degree of the Natural Minor Scale was changed half a step up,
>   forming a scale as complete as the Natural Major Scale because now it has a Leading Tone. That

**Significance (p. 223).** Defines the construction rule that produces the Harmonic Minor Scale from the Natural Minor.

## Birth of Melodic Minor Scale

> In addition to the Harmonic Minor Scale, another scale was created: the Melodic Minor Scale.
>   Initially, that scale was used only in its ascending order, because the descending order was
>   difficult to sing in tune, as it sounded a lot like the Natural Major Scale, and contained a minor
>   3rd at the end.

**Significance (p. 223).** Explains the historical asymmetric (ascending-only) origin of melodic minor.

## Bach Scale / modern melodic minor

> Currently the Melodic Minor Scale is considered by most modern authors as being equal to the
> Bach Scale, meaning that it has only one form, both ascending and descending.

**Significance (p. 224).** Establishes the book's working definition of melodic minor as a single symmetric scale.

## Origin of Harmonic Major Scale

> In the mid-twentieth century, the Russian composer Nicolai A. Rimsky-Korsakov, named
> another variation of the Natural Major Scale: Harmonic Major Scale

**Significance (p. 224).** Attributes and names the third new scale system the chapter introduces.

## Three new scale families generate modes

> Just like the natural major and natural minor scale, each one of these three new scales generate
> seven different modes, creating three groups of seven scales each. In theory, we could also
> consider them harmonic fields, however in order to avoid confusion with names, we will call
> them"scale modes".

**Significance (p. 224).** Defines the organizing principle of the chapter: three families x seven modes, called scale modes not harmonic fields.

## Function of derived chord families

> It is important to understant that we cannot use the chord families derived from these three
> groups of scale modes to guide us through songs, we can only use them to add more "colors" to
> the chords.

**Significance (p. 224).** Critical rule: these derived chord families are color/tension devices, not structural progressions.

## Harmonic Minor Im mode intervals

> Im Harmonic Minor: Root /maj 2nd /min 3rd /perf 4th /perf 5th /min 6th /maj 7th

**Significance (p. 225).** Canonical interval spelling for the Harmonic Minor Im mode (the parent scale).

## Avoid/characteristic notes for Am(maj7)

> Avoid notes: minor 6th when the chord is Am(maj7)
> Characteristic notes: major 7th

**Significance (p. 226).** Pattern example of how each derived mode is specified by avoid + characteristic notes.

## Rule: extensions as passing tones

> VERY IMPORTANT:
>
>       Not all extended notes of a chord must be in it.
>
>       Many of them should only be played as the passing tones of a melody on the chord.

**Significance (p. 227).** Stated as VERY IMPORTANT across all three scale families - extensions inform melody, not necessarily voicings.

## Visualization device: bass note on 5th/6th

> The best way to not get lost on the guitar fretboard is to /irst visualize the bass note of the
> chord. However, different from scales, we must visualize the root of the chord (bass note)
> only using the 5th and 6th strings.

**Significance (p. 228).** Core fretboard visualization rule used to navigate each scale's chord family.

## Melodic Minor Im mode intervals

> Im Melodic Minor: Root /maj 2nd /min 3rd /perf 4th /perf 5th /maj 6th /maj 7th

**Significance (p. 251).** Canonical interval spelling for the Melodic Minor Im mode (the parent scale).

## Altered Scale intervals (VII)

> VII Altered : Root /min 2nd /aug 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /min 6th /min 7th

**Significance (p. 251).** Defines the Altered Scale (G7alt) as the VIIth mode of Melodic Minor - load-bearing for jazz dominant theory.

## bIII Lydian Augmented intervals

> bIII Lydian Augmented: Root /maj 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /aug 5th /maj 6th /maj 7th

**Significance (p. 251).** Canonical spelling of Lydian Augmented as bIII of Melodic Minor - signature jazz major7#5 source.

## IV Lydian Dominant intervals

> IV Lydian Dominant: Root /maj 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /perf 5th /maj 6th /min 7th

**Significance (p. 251).** Defines Lydian Dominant (7#11) as the IV of Melodic Minor - core jazz dominant device.

## Harmonic Major I mode intervals

> I Harmonic Major: Root /maj 2nd /maj 3rd /perf 4th /perf 5th /min 6th /maj 7th

**Significance (p. 277).** Canonical interval spelling for the Harmonic Major parent scale - major scale with b6.

## Harmonic Major bVI Lydian Augmented #2/#5

> bVI Lydian Augmented (#2/ #5): Root /aug 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /aug 5th /maj 6th /maj 7th

**Significance (p. 277).** Defines the doubly-augmented mode that emerges from Harmonic Major bVI - distinct from the Melodic Minor Lydian Augmented.

## Harmonic Major VIIm Locrian bb7

**Significance (p. 277).** Defines the fully diminished 7th mode of Harmonic Major - source for o7(b13) and (11) chord colors.

## Study device: same-key practice

> a) Play the harmonic /ields in the same key until you feel comfortable.

**Significance (p. 228).** First and primary practice rule applied identically to all three scale families.

## Study device: rhythm-based practice

> b) Always study with rhythm (funk, swing, etc). It helps you to memorize things faster.

**Significance (p. 228).** Pedagogical rule: rhythmic context aids memorization of the chord families.

> d) To make visualization easier, choose chords that are close to each other, using only the
>    6th and 5th string chords. The 4th string chords should be used as soon as you get

**Significance (p. 228).** Method rule constraining how the learner approaches each new scale family on the fretboard.
